Steps.Name: VPN Client Installed
Steps.Description: Checks that a supported enterprise VPN client is installed on this device.
Steps.UserFeedback: Looking for an installed VPN client...

ResolutionText.Failed:
1. Contact IT to request VPN client installation via the software deployment portal
2. For AnyConnect/Secure Client: download from the corporate self-service portal
3. For GlobalProtect: obtain the installer from IT or the company intranet
4. Ensure you are not blocking installation via a third-party security tool

TestResultText.Passed: A supported enterprise VPN client is installed.
TestResultText.Failed: No VPN client was detected. You will not be able to connect to corporate resources remotely.
TestResultText.Warning: VPN client presence is uncertain. Check Apps & Features manually.

Steps.Name: VPN Service Running
Steps.Description: Confirms the underlying VPN service (e.g., AnyConnect, GlobalProtect) is started.
Steps.ResolutionScript: $vpnServices = @('vpnagent','csc_vpnagent','pangps','PanGPA','ZSATunnel','IVpnSvc','PulseSecure'); foreach ($s in $vpnServices) { $svc = Get-Service $s -EA SilentlyContinue; if ($svc -and $svc.Status -ne 'Running') { Start-Service $s -EA SilentlyContinue } }

ResolutionText.Failed:
1. Press Win+R, type 'services.msc'
2. Locate your VPN client service (e.g., Cisco AnyConnect, GlobalProtect)
3. Right-click and select Start
4. If it won't start, try reinstalling the VPN client
5. Reboot and try again — some VPN services require a clean system state
ResolutionText.Warning: VPN service could not be identified. Ensure your VPN client is installed and configured correctly.

TestResultText.Passed: VPN client service is running and ready.
TestResultText.Failed: VPN service is stopped. The VPN client cannot establish a tunnel.
TestResultText.Warning: No recognized VPN service was found. Verify the VPN client is installed.

Steps.Name: Active VPN Tunnel
Steps.Description: Checks whether a VPN tunnel is currently established.
Steps.UserFeedback: Checking for an active VPN tunnel...
Steps.ResolutionScript: $apps = @('vpnui','PANGPA','ZSAClient'); foreach ($a in $apps) { $p = Get-Command $a -EA SilentlyContinue; if ($p) { Start-Process $p.Source; break } }

ResolutionText.Warning:
1. Open your VPN client application
2. Select the corporate VPN gateway/profile
3. Enter your credentials (and MFA if required)
4. If connection fails, check the VPN client logs for error codes
5. Ensure your internet connection is working before connecting to VPN

TestResultText.Passed: A VPN tunnel is active. You are connected to the corporate network.
TestResultText.Warning: No active VPN tunnel detected. Corporate resources requiring VPN will not be accessible.

Steps.Name: Split Tunneling Check
Steps.Description: Detects whether split tunneling is in use, which may allow corporate traffic to bypass VPN.
Steps.UserFeedback: Analyzing routing table for split tunnel configuration...

ResolutionText.Warning:
Split tunneling may be a policy decision by IT. If you are experiencing issues accessing corporate resources:
1. Contact IT to verify if split tunneling is intentional
2. If it is not policy, request a full-tunnel VPN profile
3. Do not attempt to modify VPN profiles without IT authorization

TestResultText.Passed: Full-tunnel VPN is active. All traffic is routed through the corporate VPN.
TestResultText.Failed: Unable to determine tunnel routing mode.
TestResultText.Warning: Split tunneling is in effect. Only some traffic is routed through VPN.

Steps.Name: DNS Resolves Internal Resources
Steps.Description: Tests whether internal corporate DNS names can be resolved over the VPN.
Steps.UserFeedback: Testing internal DNS resolution over VPN...

ResolutionText.Warning:
1. Verify your VPN is fully connected (not just the service running)
2. Run 'ipconfig /flushdns' to clear cached DNS
3. Confirm the VPN assigns a DNS server from the corporate network (ipconfig /all)
4. If split-DNS is misconfigured, contact IT to correct the VPN DNS suffix list

TestResultText.Passed: Internal corporate DNS names are resolving successfully over VPN.
TestResultText.Failed: Internal DNS resolution failed. Corporate resources may not be accessible.
TestResultText.Warning: Unable to resolve known internal hostnames. VPN DNS may not be configured correctly.

Steps.Name: VPN Authentication (Certificate)
Steps.Description: Checks that a valid machine or user certificate for VPN authentication exists in the certificate store.

ResolutionText.Warning:
1. Open Certificate Manager (certmgr.msc) and check Personal > Certificates
2. If no valid certificate exists, request enrollment via: certlm.msc > Personal > All Tasks > Request New Certificate
3. If certificate-based auth is not required by your VPN profile, this warning can be ignored
4. For expired certificates, contact IT to trigger auto-enrollment via Group Policy

TestResultText.Passed: A valid client authentication certificate is present for VPN use.
TestResultText.Warning: No valid or soon-expiring client auth certificate found. Certificate-based VPN may fail.

Steps.Name: Intranet Reachability via VPN
Steps.Description: Attempts an HTTP check to a known corporate intranet address to confirm end-to-end VPN access.
Steps.UserFeedback: Testing HTTP connectivity to corporate intranet...

ResolutionText.Warning:
1. Confirm VPN is connected and you have a tunnel IP assigned
2. Verify your intranet URL with IT — the default targets in this test may not match your environment
3. Check if a proxy is required to reach the intranet even over VPN
4. Try opening the intranet URL manually in a browser while on VPN

TestResultText.Passed: Corporate intranet is accessible over VPN.
TestResultText.Warning: No response from known intranet addresses. VPN may be connected but routing or DNS may be misconfigured.
